title: Duotone Evo vs Rebel — Which Kite Is Right for You?
meta_title: Duotone Evo vs Rebel 2026 — Kite Comparison
meta_description: Duotone Evo vs Rebel: side-by-side comparison of specs, riding style, wind range & who each kite is for. Expert breakdown from Surf Store.
meta_keywords: duotone evo vs rebel,evo or rebel,duotone kite comparison
body_html:
Evo vs Rebel — The Quick Answer

The Evo is Duotone's all-around kite — stable, forgiving, and capable across every riding style. The Rebel is the power/wave kite — direct, grunty, and built for riders who want a connected, sheeting feel. If you ride one kite for everything, buy the Evo. If you ride waves or want raw power, buy the Rebel.

Side-by-Side Comparison

FeatureEvo SLSRebel SLS
Kite TypeDelta HybridC-Hybrid
Best ForFreeride, progression, all-roundWave, powered freeride, big air
Wind RangeWide — covers 8–10 knots per sizeModerate — optimised for powered riding
Bar PressureLight to moderateModerate to heavy
RelaunchExcellent — almost self-launchingGood — may need a pull in light wind
DepowerMassive range — very safeGood range but less than Evo
Boost/Big AirGood — smooth liftExcellent — explosive pop and hangtime
Wave RidingAdequate — drifts OKExcellent — pivots fast, drifts naturally
Beginner FriendlyYesNo — too direct for first kite
Price (9m)~€1,200–€1,400~€1,200–€1,400

Who Should Buy the Evo?

The Evo is the right choice if you: ride in variable conditions, want one kite that does everything well, are still progressing, prioritise safety and easy relaunch, or ride foil and twin-tip in the same session. The Evo is the most versatile kite in the Duotone range — and arguably the most versatile kite on the market.

Who Should Buy the Rebel?

The Rebel is for you if: you ride waves and want a kite that pivots and drifts, you prefer a direct and powerful feel on the bar, you chase big air and want maximum boost, or you're an experienced rider who wants character over comfort.

Can I Use Both in a Quiver?

Yes — many riders run an Evo as their larger kite (11–13m for light wind) and a Rebel as their smaller kite (7–9m for powered conditions). This gives you the Evo's easy handling in marginal wind and the Rebel's power when conditions fire.

FAQ

Q: Which is better for a second kite after lessons?
A: The Evo — hands down. It's the safest, most forgiving kite for progression.

Q: Is the Rebel good for foiling?
A: Functional but not ideal. The Evo's lighter bar pressure and wider depower make it better for kite foiling.

Q: Evo SLS vs Evo D/LAB — what's the difference?
A: D/LAB is the premium construction — lighter, stiffer canopy for better performance. SLS is the best value for most riders.

title: Best Windsurf Sails 2026 — Top Picks by Category
meta_title: Best Windsurf Sails 2026 — Expert Top Picks
meta_description: Best windsurf sails for 2026: top picks for freeride, wave, race & foil. Duotone, NeilPryde, Gaastra & Point-7 compared.
meta_keywords: best windsurf sail 2026,top windsurf sails,best freeride sail,best wave sail
body_html:
Best Windsurf Sails 2026 — Our Expert Picks

We've tested the 2026 sail ranges from Duotone, NeilPryde, Gaastra, and Point-7 on the water in real conditions. Here are our top picks by category — chosen for performance, build quality, and value.

Best Freeride Sail: Gaastra Matrix

The Matrix is the best value freeride sail on the market. Easy to rig, wide wind range, forgiving power delivery, and build quality that matches sails at higher price points. If you sail 20–50 days a year and want one sail that handles everything, the Matrix is our top recommendation.

Runner-up: NeilPryde Fusion — slightly higher performance with a narrower wind range.

Best Race Sail: Point-7 AC-K

The AC-K delivers PWA-level speed at a price 20–30% below Duotone Super_Star SLS and NeilPryde RS:Flight. Andrea Cucchi's racing experience is embedded in every panel. For club racers and speed enthusiasts, this is the best performance per euro in windsurfing.

Runner-up: Duotone Super_Star SLS — the PWA benchmark, premium price.

Best Wave Sail: Duotone Super_Hero SLS

Lightweight, reactive, and built for down-the-line wave riding. The Super_Hero delivers instant response to sheet movements and rotates effortlessly through tacks and jibes in breaking surf. Available in sizes from 3.0m to 5.7m.

Runner-up: Point-7 Salt — compact and poppy, excellent in onshore conditions.

Best Foil Sail: Duotone Warp_Foil

Purpose-built for windsurf foiling with a narrow head, short luff, and controlled power delivery. The Warp_Foil lifts early and maintains smooth, consistent drive once flying — exactly what foil sailing demands.

Runner-up: Point-7 AC-F — race-oriented foil sail with more power.

Best Beginner Sail: Gaastra Boost

Forgiving, easy to rig, and designed specifically for riders learning to plane and use the harness. The Boost delivers smooth power without sudden gusts — building confidence instead of fear.

FAQ

Q: How much should I spend on a windsurf sail?
A: €400–€600 for entry level, €600–€900 for mid-range freeride, €900–€1,400 for race/premium. A quality mid-range sail like the Gaastra Matrix costs around €500–€700.

Q: How long do windsurf sails last?
A: 3–5 seasons with proper care. Store rolled (not folded), rinse after saltwater use, and avoid prolonged UV exposure.

Q: Should I buy brand-matched masts?
A: Ideally yes for optimal sail shape. But any IMCS-rated mast at the correct specification will work.

title: Cabrinha Switchblade vs Duotone Evo 2026
meta_description: Cabrinha Switchblade vs Duotone Evo: detailed comparison of the two most popular all-round kites. Which is best for you?
meta_keywords: switchblade vs evo,cabrinha vs duotone,best all round kite 2026
body_html:
The Two Most Popular Kites in the World

The Cabrinha Switchblade and Duotone Evo are the best-selling all-round kites globally — and for good reason. Both deliver wide wind range, easy relaunch, forgiving depower, and all-around performance. But they achieve it differently, and those differences matter depending on your riding style.

Head-to-Head Comparison

FeatureCabrinha SwitchbladeDuotone Evo SLS
DesignDelta Hybrid (SLE)Delta Hybrid
Struts33
Bar PressureMedium — linear feelLight-medium — responsive
RelaunchExcellentExcellent
Wind RangeVery wideVery wide
BoostStrong — floaty hangtimeStrong — smooth lift
Turning SpeedMedium — deliberateMedium-fast — reactive
Low-End PowerExcellent — generates power earlyVery good
High-End ControlVery good — stable at top endExcellent — smooth depower
FoilingGoodVery good
Beginner FriendlyYesYes

The Switchblade Edge

The Switchblade generates more low-end power than the Evo — it planes riders earlier and pulls harder in marginal wind. If your spot averages 12–18 knots and you want every possible minute on the water, the Switchblade's power generation gives you an edge. It also has a distinctive, floaty hangtime during jumps that many riders prefer.

The Evo Edge

The Evo turns faster and has lighter bar pressure — making it more responsive during dynamic riding, transitions, and kite loops. Its depower range is marginally wider, giving slightly more safety margin in gusts. For foiling, the Evo's lighter touch is preferred. The Evo also has the edge in build quality consistency — Duotone's SLS construction is exceptionally refined.

The Verdict

You can't go wrong with either kite. If you value low-end power and floaty jumps: Switchblade. If you value responsiveness, lighter bar feel, and versatility across foil/twin-tip: Evo. Both are excellent progression kites that you'll keep in your quiver for years.

FAQ

Q: Which is cheaper?
A: Prices are comparable. The Switchblade is sometimes €50–100 less depending on retailer. The Evo SLS is the standard comparison — D/LAB is more expensive.

Q: Can I switch from one to the other easily?
A: Yes — both kites fly similarly. You'll adapt within 10 minutes. The main difference is bar pressure and turning speed.

Q: Which do Surf Store staff ride?
A: Split about 60/40 in favour of the Evo, but several team members swear by the Switchblade for light-wind days.

title: Best Wetsuits for Kitesurfing 2026 — Top 5
meta_description: Best wetsuits for kitesurfing 2026: our top 5 picks tested on the water. ION & NeilPryde compared. Expert fit advice from Surf Store.
meta_keywords: best wetsuit kitesurfing,kite wetsuit,best kite wetsuit 2026,ion wetsuit review
body_html:
What Makes a Good Kite Wetsuit?

Kitesurfing puts unique demands on a wetsuit. You need maximum arm freedom for bar control, reinforced knees for board starts, secure wrist seals that don't flush during crashes, and stretch across the shoulders for overhead kite handling. A wetsuit designed for surfing may not meet these requirements.

Our Top 5 Kite Wetsuits for 2026

1. ION Seek Amp 4/3mm — Best Overall

Front-zip entry, thermal lining throughout, pre-bent knees and arms, and liquid-seal wrist cuffs. This is the suit our team reaches for on 90% of sessions. Warm enough for 12–16°C water without being bulky. The arm panels use thinner neoprene (3mm) for unrestricted bar control while the torso stays at 4mm for warmth.

2. ION Element 4/3mm — Best Value

80% of the Seek Amp's performance at 60% of the price. Back-zip entry (easier to get on), quality neoprene, sealed seams, and reinforced knee panels. The go-to recommendation for riders who want quality without the premium.

3. ION Seek Amp 5/4mm — Best for Cold Water

Same construction as the 4/3 but with added thickness and an integrated hood option for water below 12°C. The thermal fleece lining retains heat remarkably well — riders report comfortable 2-hour sessions in 8–10°C water.

4. NeilPryde Wizard 4/3mm — Best Windsurf Crossover

NeilPryde's windsurf heritage shows in the Wizard's arm cut — wider panels that accommodate the overhead movements of both windsurfing and kitesurfing. GBS seams and 3D knee construction add durability and comfort.

5. ION Base 3/2mm — Best Budget Summer Suit

Clean construction, reliable warmth for 16–22°C water, and a price that won't break the bank. Perfect for riders who kite 10–20 days per year in warmer months. Back-zip for easy entry.

Size Matters More Than Brand

The best wetsuit in the world is useless if it doesn't fit. A loose suit flushes with cold water and loses all insulation. A tight suit restricts movement and exhausts you. Our advice: try before you buy, or use the manufacturer's detailed size chart with actual body measurements — not generic S/M/L guessing.

FAQ

Q: 3/2mm or 4/3mm for European kitesurfing?
A: 4/3mm covers the widest range. 3/2mm is fine for Mediterranean summer only. If you buy one suit for year-round EU use, choose 4/3mm.

Q: How often should I replace my kite wetsuit?
A: Every 2–3 seasons with regular use (50+ sessions/year). Replace when seams start leaking or neoprene loses its stretch.

Q: Back-zip or chest-zip for kitesurfing?
A: Chest-zip or front-zip for cold water (warmer seal). Back-zip for convenience and ease of dressing. Most serious kiters prefer chest-zip.
